example: SELECT foo FROM regexp_split_to_table('the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog', '\s+') AS foo;
foo ------- the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog (9 rows) SELECT regexp_split_to_array('the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog', '\s+');
regexp_split_to_array ----------------------------------------------- {the,quick,brown,fox,jumps,over,the,lazy,dog} (1 row) SELECT foo FROM regexp_split_to_table('the quick brown fox', '\s*') AS foo;

example: SELECT foo FROM regexp_split_to_table('the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog', '\s+') AS foo;
foo ------- the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog (9 rows) SELECT regexp_split_to_array('the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog', '\s+');
regexp_split_to_array ----------------------------------------------- {the,quick,brown,fox,jumps,over,the,lazy,dog} (1 row) SELECT foo FROM regexp_split_to_table('the quick brown fox', '\s*') AS foo;

regexp_split_to_array ----------------------------------------------- {the,quick,brown,fox,jumps,over,the,lazy,dog} (1 row) SELECT foo FROM regexp_split_to_table('the quick brown fox', '\s*') AS foo;

detail: [DESCRIPTION]: <sep>[SYNTAX]: generate_series<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: <sep>[RETURN]: <sep>[DEMO]: SELECT * FROM generate_series(2,4); ; generate_series ----------------- 2 3 4 (3 rows) SELECT * FROM generate_series(5,1,-2); ; generate_series ----------------- 5 3 1 (3 rows) SELECT * FROM generate_series(4,3); ; generate_series ----------------- (0 rows) SELECT generate_series(1.1, 4, 1.3); ; generate_series ----------------- 1.1 2.4 3.7 (3 rows) -- this example relies on the date-plus-integer operator: SELECT current_date + s.a AS dates FROM generate_series(0,14,7) AS s(a); ; dates ------------ 2004-02-05 2004-02-12 2004-02-19 (3 rows) SELECT * FROM generate_series('2008-03-01 00:00'::timestamp, '2008-03-04 12:00', '10 hours'); ; -- basic usage: SELECT generate_subscripts('{NULL,1,NULL,2}'::int[], 1) AS s; ; s --- 1 2 3 4 (4 rows) -- presenting an array, the subscript and the subscripted -- value requires a subquery: SELECT * FROM arrays; ; a -------------------- {-1,-2} {100,200,300} (2 rows) SELECT a AS array, s AS subscript, a[s] AS value FROM (SELECT generate_subscripts(a, 1) AS s, a FROM arrays) foo; ; array | subscript | value ---------------+-----------+------- {-1,-2} | 1 | -1 {-1,-2} | 2 | -2 {100,200,300} | 1 | 100 {100,200,300} | 2 | 200 {100,200,300} | 3 | 300 (5 rows) -- unnest a 2D array: CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION unnest2(anyarray) RETURNS SETOF anyelement AS $$ select $1[i][j] from generate_subscripts($1,1) g1(i), generate_subscripts($1,2) g2(j); ; CREATE FUNCTION SELECT * FROM unnest2(ARRAY[[1,2],[3,4]]); ; -- set returning function WITH ORDINALITY: SELECT * FROM pg_ls_dir('.') WITH ORDINALITY AS t(ls,n);
example: generate_series ----------------- 1.1 2.4 3.7 (3 rows) -- this example relies on the date-plus-integer operator: SELECT current_date + s.a AS dates FROM generate_series(0,14,7) AS s(a);
s --- 1 2 3 4 (4 rows) -- presenting an array, the subscript and the subscripted -- value requires a subquery: SELECT * FROM arrays;
a -------------------- {-1,-2} {100,200,300} (2 rows) SELECT a AS array, s AS subscript, a[s] AS value FROM (SELECT generate_subscripts(a, 1) AS s, a FROM arrays) foo;

detail: [DESCRIPTION]: <sep>[SYNTAX]: xmax<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: <sep>[RETURN]: One past the highest completed transaction ID. detail: [DESCRIPTION]: <sep>[SYNTAX]: xip_list<sep>[ARGUMENTS]: <sep>[RETURN]: Transactions in progress at the time of the snapshot. A transaction ID that is xmin <= X < xmax and not in this list was already completed at the time of the snapshot, and thus is either visible or dead according to its commit status. This list does not include the transaction IDs of subtransactions.<sep>[DEMO]: SELECT; ; SELECT has_table_privilege('myschema.mytable', 'select'); ; SELECT has_table_privilege('joe', 'mytable', 'INSERT, SELECT WITH GRANT OPTION'); ; INSERT; ; UPDATE; ; SELECT has_function_privilege('joeuser', 'myfunc(int, text)', 'execute'); ; DELETE; ; SELECT relname FROM pg_class WHERE pg_table_is_visible(oid); ; SELECT pg_type_is_visible('myschema.widget'::regtype); ; SELECT currval(pg_get_serial_sequence('sometable', 'id')); ; SELECT pg_typeof(33); ; pg_typeof ----------- integer SELECT typlen FROM pg_type WHERE oid = pg_typeof(33);
